findings.title: Container runs as root user
findings.description: Running a container as root is a serious security misconfiguration. If an attacker compromises the application, they automatically gain root access to the container, making container escape attacks much easier.
findings.fix: Add 'USER appuser' at the end of your Dockerfile (create the user first with RUN adduser).

findings.title: Latest tag used for base image
findings.description: The 'latest' tag is not a fixed version — it changes over time. An image that is secure today may contain vulnerabilities tomorrow. It also makes builds impossible to reproduce consistently.
findings.fix: Use a specific tag, e.g. FROM python:3.11.9-slim instead of FROM python:latest

findings.title: Hardcoded password or API key in ENV
findings.description: Secrets hardcoded in a Dockerfile become part of the Docker image layer. Anyone who pulls the image can read them using 'docker history' or by inspecting the image layers directly.
findings.fix: Use Docker Secrets or pass environment variables at runtime. Never hardcode secrets in a Dockerfile.

findings.title: ADD used instead of COPY
findings.description: The ADD instruction has hidden behaviour: it automatically extracts archives and can fetch files from URLs. This can lead to unexpected results and potential security issues. COPY is more explicit and safer.
findings.fix: Replace ADD with COPY for copying local files. Only use ADD if you specifically need archive extraction.

findings.title: Missing HEALTHCHECK instruction
findings.description: Without a HEALTHCHECK instruction, Docker cannot determine whether the application inside the container is actually healthy. A container may show as 'running' while the application has crashed.
findings.fix: Add a HEALTHCHECK instruction, e.g: HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s CMD curl -f http://localhost:8080/health || exit 1

findings.title: apt-get without --no-install-recommends
findings.description: Installing packages without --no-install-recommends pulls in unnecessary packages that increase the attack surface. More packages means more potential vulnerabilities.

findings.title: Privileged mode enabled
findings.description: privileged: true gives the container nearly the same access to the host as a root user. An attacker who compromises such a container can easily perform a container escape attack and take control of the host machine.
findings.fix: Remove 'privileged: true'. If specific capabilities are needed, use 'cap_add' for only those Linux capabilities required.

findings.title: Docker socket mounted into container
findings.description: Mounting /var/run/docker.sock gives the container full control over the Docker daemon. An attacker inside the container can create new privileged containers, mount the host filesystem, and effectively take over the entire host.
findings.fix: Never mount the Docker socket into production containers. If Docker-in-Docker is needed, use alternatives like Kaniko.

findings.title: Missing resource limits (CPU/memory)
findings.description: Without resource limits, a single container can consume all host resources and bring down other services — known as a resource exhaustion or noisy neighbor problem.
findings.fix:
Add limits under deploy.resources:
  limits:
    cpus: '0.5'
    memory: 512M

findings.title: Restart policy not defined
findings.description: Without a restart policy, containers will not automatically restart after a crash or system reboot. This is not a direct security issue but affects service availability.
findings.fix: Add 'restart: unless-stopped' or 'restart: always' for production services.
